ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general uttapam entry (FOODON_03302051); no cheese-topped variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers plain uttapam; cheese-topped variant not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for uttapam or cheese uttapam. None assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium cheese uttapam weighing approximately 150 g, as typically served at a South Indian restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Uttapam (plain) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, adapted for cheese topping using USDA FoodData Central Cheddar Cheese (FDC ID 173414)
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Plain uttapam values sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition). Cheese contribution estimated by adding approximately 15 g cheddar cheese per 100 g of finished product, using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cheddar cheese data (FDC ID 173414). Final per-100g values represent a weighted blend of plain uttapam batter (~85%) and cheddar cheese (~15%) as a reasonable approximation of a cheese-topped uttapam as served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of South Indian fermented foods — Uttapam (plain fermented rice-lentil pancake), analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / peer-reviewed literature (Shobana et al., 2007, European Journal of Clinical Nutrition)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for cheese uttapam found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 51 is derived from Shobana et al. (2007), which reported GI values for South Indian fermented rice-lentil foods including idli (GI ~46) and dosa (GI ~67). Uttapam, sharing the same fermented rice-urad dal batter as idli and dosa but cooked thicker, is estimated at GI 51 (low). Addition of cheese (low GI dairy) does not materially raise the GI. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (51 × 41.25) / 100 ≈ 21.1 for a 150 g serving containing approximately 41.25 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~280 mg per 100 g; ~420 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit cheese uttapam to one piece per meal and avoid additional high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or salty chutneys, as the combined sodium load from cheese and fermented batter can approach 30% of the recommended daily sodium limit per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~21 per 150 g serving) and refined rice carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Persons with Type 2 Diabetes should consume cheese uttapam in controlled portions (one piece) and pair it with high-fibre accompaniments such as sambar or vegetable chutney to blunt the postprandial glycemic response, given the moderate glycemic load of approximately 21 per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cheese (~2.1 g per 100 g) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit cheese uttapam to occasional consumption, as the saturated fat contributed by the cheese topping (~3.2 g per 150 g serving) and moderate sodium content are relevant to lipid management and blood pressure control; substituting with a lower-fat cheese or reducing the cheese quantity is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese uttapam is a South Indian fermented rice and lentil pancake topped with grated or melted cheese, commonly served at South Indian and Indian banana leaf restaurants in Singapore. The base batter is made from a fermented blend of rice and urad dal (black gram lentils), which is poured onto a hot griddle and cooked until the underside is golden and the surface is set, then topped with cheese that melts into the surface. The result is a thick, soft, slightly sour pancake with a creamy, savoury cheese layer. Uttapam differs from dosa in that it is thicker and cooked more slowly, giving it a spongy interior. The cheese variant is a modern adaptation popular in Singapore and urban India, appealing to younger diners and those seeking a richer flavour profile. Nutritionally, cheese uttapam provides moderate energy (~175 kcal per 100 g), a useful combination of plant protein from lentils and dairy protein from cheese, and a moderate glycemic index of approximately 51, placing it in the low-GI category. The fermentation process improves digestibility and increases B-vitamin bioavailability. The sodium content is moderate (~280 mg per 100 g) due to salt in the batter and the cheese, making portion control relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is typically served with coconut chutney and sambar. In Singapore, it is classified as a South Indian dish and is available at banana leaf restaurants and Indian hawker stalls.
common_user_queries: How many calories in cheese uttapam?
Is cheese uttapam suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of uttapam?
What is the difference between uttapam and dosa?
How much sodium is in cheese uttapam?
Where can I find cheese uttapam in Singapore?

confidence_reason: Plain uttapam values are sourced from ICMR-NIN, an approved primary source for Indian dishes. The cheese contribution is estimated using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cheddar data, a secondary approved source. No exact cheese uttapam entry exists in any approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature on South Indian fermented foods. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Cuisine classification: The input JSON listed cuisine as 'Malaysian' and food_group as 'Dairy and eggs'. Uttapam is a South Indian dish; the restaurant (Singapore Banana Leaf Restaurant) serves South Indian cuisine. Cuisine corrected to 'South Indian' and food_group corrected to 'Grains and staples'. 2. Nutrient values are adapted estimates: plain uttapam from ICMR-NIN blended with cheddar cheese contribution from USDA FoodData Central. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: uttapam batter and cheddar cheese contain negligible vitamin C; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA sources. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for uttapam; cheddar contribution is minor and not separately quantified in this adaptation. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for uttapam. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for uttapam. 7. GI set to 51 (low) based on analogous estimate from Shobana et al. (2007) for South Indian fermented rice-lentil foods. 8. Glycemic load per serving = (51 × 41.25) / 100 ≈ 21.1 for a 150 g serving. 9. FoodOn URI FOODON_03302051 is used as closest parent for plain uttapam; no cheese-topped variant is separately coded. 10. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is used as closest parent for plain uttapam. 11. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean dish context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in Singapore. 12. The input cuisine_tags ('Malaysian', 'Noodle', 'Fried Rice', 'Roti Prata') appear to be restaurant-level tags from field survey and do not accurately describe this dish; they have not been carried forward.
